title: LoRA
lesson:
LoRA (short for Low-Rank Adaptation) is the trick that made fine-tuning cheap enough to do on a single gaming GPU instead of a room full of servers. Instead of editing the whole model, you bolt on a tiny set of extra parameters and train only those. The big model stays frozen; you teach a small add-on.

The problem LoRA solves

A model's knowledge lives in its weights — huge grids of numbers (called matrices) that get multiplied against your text as it flows through the network. A 7-billion-parameter model has billions of these numbers. Classic fine-tuning nudges every single one of them. To do that you must hold the whole model, plus a full set of training bookkeeping (gradients and optimiser state — roughly the model's size again, several times over) in GPU memory. That is why full fine-tuning a 7B model can need 60GB or more of VRAM and serious hardware.

Here is the key insight behind LoRA: when you adapt a model to a new task, the change you actually need is small and simple — even though the model is enormous. You do not need to repaint the whole house to change one room.

Analogy 1: sticky notes on a textbook

Imagine a 2,000-page reference textbook (the frozen model). You do not own a printing press, so you cannot reprint it. But you can add sticky notes in the margins that say "in our company, do it this way." The book underneath never changes; your notes ride on top and steer how it gets read. LoRA's add-on is that thin layer of sticky notes — a few megabytes against a several-gigabyte book.

Analogy 2: a thin lens clipped over a camera

The camera body and its expensive sensor are the frozen model. Clip a small correction lens in front and the picture shifts — sharper here, warmer there — without touching the sensor. Swap lenses and you get a different look from the same camera. A LoRA is that clip-on lens: small, removable, and you can keep a drawer full of them for different jobs.

The "low-rank" idea, in plain English

A weight update is itself a big grid of numbers. LoRA's bet is that this update is low-rank — a fancy way of saying it can be rebuilt from two skinny grids instead of one fat one. Picture a 1000×1000 square (a million numbers). Replace it with a 1000×8 strip times an 8×1000 strip. Multiply those two and you get back a 1000×1000 grid — but you only had to store and train 16,000 numbers, not a million. That little number 8 is the rank (often written r): how skinny the strips are. The two strips are usually called A and B.

During training the original weight W is frozen. The model uses W + (B×A), and only A and B get gradient updates (their numbers get adjusted). At the end you have a tiny file — often just a few megabytes — called the adapter.

from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model

config = LoraConfig(
    r=8,                 # rank: the skinniness of the strips
    lora_alpha=16,       # scaling on the update
    target_modules=["q_proj", "v_proj"],  # which layers to adapt
    lora_dropout=0.05,
)
model = get_peft_model(base_model, config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: ~4M  ||  all params: ~7B  ||  trainable%: 0.06

That last line is the whole story: you are training roughly 0.06% of the model. This is why LoRA belongs to the family called PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning), and why it sits right next to plain adapter tuning.

Why this is a big deal in practice

  • Cheap: trains on one consumer GPU; fast to run because there are few numbers to update.
  • Tiny outputs: a 7B base model is ~14GB, but its LoRA adapter might be 10–50MB. You ship the small file, not a whole new model.
  • Swappable: keep the base model loaded once and hot-swap different adapters — one for legal tone, one for code, one for a customer's brand voice.
  • Mergeable: for deployment you can fold the adapter back into the weights (W + B×A becomes a new W) so there is zero extra cost at inference time.

The famous companion is QLoRA, which first squeezes the frozen base model down with quantization (storing weights in fewer bits) so even a 70B model fits on a single card while LoRA does the learning on top. Tools like Unsloth, Axolotl, and the TRL library turn a LoRA job into a short config file rather than a research project.

💡 Two dials matter most: r (rank — capacity of the add-on) and lora_alpha (how strongly the add-on speaks). A common starting point is r=8 or 16 with alpha set to roughly twice r.
mentalModel: LoRA freezes the giant model like a printed textbook and trains a thin stack of sticky-note adapters in the margins. Two skinny matrices stand in for one huge weight update — so you tune about 0.1% of the parameters, save a few-megabyte file, and clip it on or off at will.
mistakes: Thinking LoRA changes the base model. It does not. The base weights are frozen; the learning lives in a separate adapter. Delete the adapter and you are back to the original model exactly.

Cranking the rank r sky-high "to be safe." Bigger r means more trainable numbers, more memory, and a bigger chance of overfitting (memorising your small dataset instead of learning the pattern). Start low (8 or 16) and only raise it if quality is clearly capped.

Forgetting to scale lora_alpha with r. The effective strength is roughly alpha/r. Change r but leave alpha fixed and you silently change how loud the adapter speaks, which can wreck a run that 'worked yesterday'.

Adapting too few layers. Old guides only touched the attention query/value layers. Adapting more modules (including the MLP/feed-forward layers) usually helps, especially for harder tasks. Check what your tool targets by default.

Expecting LoRA to teach big new knowledge. A small adapter is great at style, format, and behaviour, but a thin update cannot pour in a whole new field of facts. For fresh knowledge prefer RAG or continued pretraining.

Mismatching the adapter and base at load time. An adapter is trained against one specific base model and chat template. Load it onto a different base (or the wrong quantization) and you get garbage or an outright shape-mismatch error.
exercise:
Using Hugging Face PEFT and the TRL library, fine-tune a small base model (say a 1B–3B Llama or Qwen) on a tiny dataset of ~50 examples teaching one narrow behaviour — for instance, always replying in a cheerful, emoji-free customer-support tone. Set r=8, lora_alpha=16, train for 1–3 epochs, then run print_trainable_parameters() and note what percentage of the model you actually trained. Save the adapter and check its file size on disk.

Stretch goal: Train a second adapter on the same base for a totally different style (e.g. a terse legal tone). Then load the base once and hot-swap between the two adapters on the same prompt, proving you can keep one heavy base model in memory and switch personalities by clipping on a different few-megabyte lens.
solution: Hint: Wrap the base model with get_peft_model(base, LoraConfig(...)), hand it to TRL's SFTTrainer, and train. print_trainable_parameters() should report well under 1% trainable — that is the win. The saved adapter (via model.save_pretrained("my-adapter")) will be tiny, often 10–50MB, because it stores only the A and B matrices, not the billions of frozen weights. For the stretch goal, load the base once, then call model.load_adapter(path) for each style and switch the active one with model.set_adapter(name); the heavy base never reloads, so swapping is near-instant. To deploy a single style with zero overhead, merge_and_unload() folds B×A back into W.
summary: LoRA freezes the giant base model and trains two skinny matrices (A and B) that stand in for a full weight update, exploiting the fact that task-specific changes are 'low-rank' (simple). You tune a fraction of a percent of the parameters, ship a few-megabyte adapter instead of a whole new model, and can swap, merge, or stack adapters at will. It is the default cheap fine-tuning method and the foundation under QLoRA.

keyTerms.def: A fine-tuning method that freezes the original model and trains a small add-on made of two skinny matrices, so you tune a tiny fraction of the parameters.
The grids of numbers inside a model that get multiplied against your input as it flows through the network. They hold what the model has learned.
How skinny the two LoRA matrices are. A small rank like 8 keeps the add-on tiny; a larger rank gives it more capacity but more cost and overfitting risk.
The small saved file (often 10–50MB) containing the trained LoRA matrices. You attach it to the base model to get the new behaviour.
A scaling number that controls how strongly the adapter's update is applied. The effective strength is roughly alpha divided by rank.
Parameters that are not updated during training. In LoRA the entire base model is frozen; only the adapter learns.
The family of methods, including LoRA, that train only a small slice of a model's parameters instead of all of them.
Folding a trained adapter's update back into the base weights so the deployed model needs no extra computation at inference time.
When a model memorises its small training set instead of learning the general pattern, hurting performance on new inputs.
